It is pitiable to sce the way these Unionists conduct
themselves in West Down, a constituency of North-East
Ulstor. In the town of Dromore, in Down, it appears
that a Mr. Beattie, J.P., D.L., Ex-T.C., of Dublin, was
secking to represent the constituency as against another
candidate by the name of Lord Arthur Hill. There
were ructions at this meeting in unproclaimed Down.
A large pixce of wood was thrown at Mr. Sloan, M.P.,
who was sitting on the platform.

Mr. Beattie asked:—‘ Could it be said that in the
loyal County Down a County Down man was refused
a hearing (groans).”’

We read that Mr. Beattie, D.L., J.P., also said that
““ their conduct was cannibalistic, because it could not
be exceeded in the Cannibal Islands; but on Friday next
he would be at the head of the poll (cries of ¢ Liar ’ and
“ Never,” etc., and renewed uproar.)’”  Mr. Birrell’s
< Carrion Crow’’ is quite tame by the side of Mr.
Beattie’s ¢ Cannibals.”” Can the poor ignorant Orange-
men of West Down be so bad as to deserve Mr. Beattie’s
expression? The sooner some philanthropic Society is
started to look after these poor people the better.

Even the sight of Mr. Sloan, M.P., could not soothe
the cannibalistic breast. We read that ¢ He tried to
calm the turmoil by calling for a verse of ¢ Rule Britan-
nia,” but there was no response.’”’

When the meeting was over, ‘“ Mr. Beattie was at-
tacked, assaulted, and kicked by the crowd, and Mrs.
Beattie was also struck and assaulted.
powerless to quell the disorder, and the mob attacked
and damaged the motor car, broke and smashed the
mud-guards, lamps, etc., and otherwise damaged the
car to the extent of £20. Some of the occupants of the
car were struck on the head by missiles, and the
driver had ths utmost difficulty in making his way
through the dense crowds which lined the streets of
the town, and along the Banbridge road a number of
people were knocked down. Under cover of darkness
the mob did as they pleased, and a number of Mr.
Beattie’s prominent Banbridge supporters were escorted
under police protection through the streets to private
houses in the town. Owing to the threatening de-
meanour of the mobs those travelling on outside cars,
etc., were obliged to remain in town all night.”” Tt is
a relief, anyway, that these ‘“ loyal ’ ‘‘ cannibals’’ re-
frained from eating Mr. Beattie. =~ When does Mr.
Birrell intend to proclaim West Down?

Mr. T. W. Russell’s remarks before the Congestion
Commission concerning the ruling price of land under
Wyndham’s Act, brings home the serious state of
affeirs in which the country has been landed, the serious
state of things that it has to face. According to Mr.
T. W. Russell, taking the average rate of buying out at
25 years’ purchase by means of stock quoted at a little
over 80—that means about five extra years’ purchase, or
30 years. Ontop of this is the bonus of about
another three years’ purchase—thirty-three years’ pur-
chase in all. And even that does not exhaust all, for
there is the cost of the Land Commission to be added.
Mr. T. W. Russell suggested—and we don’t think any-
one except a greedy landlord who wants to sell would

The police were .

disagree with him—that thirty-three years’ purchase
issan impossible price for agricultural rents at the best
of times, and an utterly impossible price in the falling
market of agriculture.

Mr. Russell said:—‘“ The present actual situation is
this, that in the Land Commission there is an immense
amount of arrears in the form of agreements for salo
for which there is no money available. Are these sales
to go through, and is the money to be raised, whatever
the loan may be, and if that is so, how will the country
ultm.mtely face it? T do not wish to trouble vou with
arguing the case. I merely state my opinion, that it
will end in financial ruin if purchase is allowed to con-
tinue at a cost, one way and another, of 33 years’ pur-
chase for every acre of land sold.”” Mr. Russell sug-
gested that to meet the loss on flotation, the interest
on: the stock should be raised, and that the stock should
be so issued as not to be prejudiced in the market, as
he contends it is at present. The interest to the
tenants should be likewise raised. The tenants would
continue to expect and demand a certain reduction
from their rent when their purchasing instalments were
being fixed, and so the landlords would get fewer years’
purchase for their land.

We take the following editorial note from the
Universe (London), ‘ the oldest popular Catholic news-
paper ’: —

Our enterprising Dublin contemporary, ‘“The Leader,” has
lately celebrated its eighth birthday, and is as alert and vigorous
as ever. It does not need that one should see eye to eye with
every opinion expressed in its columns to recognize the great
part which ““The Leader” has played during the past seven
years in quickening the nearly dormant energies and strengtheping
the self-reliance of the Irish race. Its campaign was fought, as
to the initial stages,-in the face of such a mass of lethargy, that
only a bold spirit would have essayed the task. Thanks to
consistent hammering away, exposure, irony, and constructive
writing, it has already done a good deal towards restoring Irish
industry and curbing influences that make for the country’s sub-
Jection. “The Leader” has done more for temperance than
some of the temperance societies, and has exposed over and over
again the favouritism shown to Protestants by railway com-
panies, banks, etc., in Ireland. Always imbued with a loyal
Catholic spirit, the paper deserves to continue for many years
to occupy in the Irish press the position of a leader in fact as
in name.

Since last October’s great public commemoration of
Father Mathew, the Act of Parliament for the Early
€losing of Publichouses on Saturdays and Sundays has
come into force. The Temperance Demonstration in
Dublin on Sunday marked, therefore, a considerable
advance in the cause for which it stood even on that
score alone. The procession, as it wended its way from
the starting point to the place of meeting in O’Connell
Street, was a significant display in favour of the move-
ment for the practical uplifting of Ireland. Irom a
platform erected by the statue of Father Mathew
vigorous speeches were addressed to the big audience
assembled by Rev. Father Peter, Father Aloysius,
Canon Casey, Father Larkin, Father O’Reilly, from
amongst the clergy, and by Messrs. T. W. Russell, P.
J. O’Neill, etc., from amongst the laity. Mr. Willie
Redmond very justly received quite an ovation when he
rose to speak. However we may have differed, or differ,
with Mr. Willie Redmond on other matters, we heartily
recognise that his attitude on the Temperance question
calls for nothing but our respect and admiration.

In the course of his speech Mr. Redmond referred to
the criticism passed on his Temperance advocacy, and,

We have emphasised more than once or twice the
necessity for more production at this stage of the Irish
Tndustrial movement, and we are glad to note that at a
recent meeting of the Council of the Cork Development
Association this aspect of the question was under con-
sideration in relation to the financing of small existing
industries which are debarred from extension by want
of capital. Several proposals were discussed, of which
the following received general approval: —‘‘ (1) That a
number of local gentlemen of good standing should
form a committee to investigate the merits of applicants
for financial aid; (2) if satisfied that the applicant’s
business was being run on proper lines and could be
usefully extended by the possession of additional capital,
and other details concerning it proving satisfactory, the
members of the committee should (3) apply to one of
the local banks to advance an approved sum of money
to the applicant at a reasonable rate of interest; (4) the
members of the committee each making themselves re-
sponsible to the bank for a proportion of the amount
advanced, in the event of the applicant failing to repay
the amount.”” It is encouraging to note that the Coun-
cil believe a considerable number of local gentlemen will
be willing to act on this committee.

WOLFE TONE.

And thou hast been with us, in all thy glory,
It Daite &t Cliat some hundred years ago,
Ere the parties ‘ Liberal’’ and ‘‘ Tory
Had yet begun their task, to overthrow
The balances and weights of poor blind Justice,
And prime themselves with methods that disgust us.

Oh, has the powerful shield of courage left us,
And must we keep our heads in slav’ry bowed,
Have alien laws of manliness bereft us,
Does seething passion choke our people proud.
Can we not have an elevated: purpose
Without the thoughts of war and blood to warp us.

Oh, shade of one of Erin’s greatest heroes,

We proudly think of thee who loved our land.
Our rulers may be Ptolemys or Neros,

Yet can they curb the power within our hand.
Have we not got a pathway clear before us
And voices strong to raise in common chorus.

T5 Ireland’s sons who fell for England’s glory (?)
TIs RAISED a stately arch of granite stone,
By those, whose treachery so foul and gory
Drove the Dutch farmer from his freeman’s home.
To thee—who died for Erin—oh! to thee,
. We sINK a slab of stone one scarce can see.

Nine years ago ’mid clamour, pomp, and splendour,
And many a pzan and.eulogy serene,
To keep the memory of our land’s defender,
We placed a little stone at Stephen’s Green.
—We lack the spirit of determination,
And prate too much of our predestination.

We speak a lot, our tongues are never-ceasing,
Our foes they say not much, but much they do.
Their Anglicising power is fast increasing,
At every turn memorials block the view.
But thee!—we’re busy building Ireland’s fame,
‘We have not time to praise a hero’s name.

Some day, perhaps, half-slavery’s narcotic
Will from our stagnant minds evaporate,
And rid us of such patriotism—Quixotic—
As makes us to forget our truly great.
Rest none the less till then, thou glorious dead,
In effigy we yet may rear thy head. K. C.

HOME INDUSTRIES.

T is, I think, at length conceded by almost everyone
I that, except in the matter of intoxicants, the
Irish are not, as a people, in earnest about supporting
their own manufactures. No doubt a few hundred
thousands are deeply in earnest and do all they can to
help by precept and example, but the millions simply
don’t bother where the goods they buy were made, so
long as quality and price are right. This, of course,
is proved by the latest returns of the Department,
issued a few weeks ago, which show that for year 1905
'(the last available), Ireland imported manufactured
goods to the amount of 26 millions sterling, an in-
-crease, be it noted, of 2 millions over 1904. That
‘there was a sensible increase, amounting to about
1} millions, in exports of manufactured goods from
Ireland in same year is very gratifying, but it is not
at all relevant to the question being considered—do
-the Irish support their own manufactures? Only last
week we read in the papers how the horse-shoeing
-smiths of England raised a storm about Mr. Haldane’s
head because he had bought a hundred thousand sets
-of horse-shoes in America for British troop horses, a
-trifling order of some twenty thousand pounds value.

We coolly watch, each year, the dumping of 24 or 26
‘millions pounds worth of foreign (principally British)
manufactured goods on our wharves and say nothing.
We take our dumps without protest, and resultant

beating lying down. And then we complain that we
are poor. Serve us right! Now, I read an excellent
letter in the LEaber quite lately, from Rev. C. M.
O’Brien, on this subject of the apathy of the Irish.
His letter I thought most admirable, except in one
respect—the very important one of how to rouse the
people from this apathy. His suggestion was that can-
vassers be appointed fo go into the dwellings of the
people, into each house, and try to persuade everybody
to buy Irish goods only—price and quality being equal,
of course. It is a large order. In 1901 there were,
according to Thom’s Directory, 858,503 inhabited
houses in Ireland. Irom this statement, the practical
impossibility of reaching each house will become evi-
dent. No doubt the Government touch over 500,000
separate homesteads each year, when finding data for
their agricultural returns, but they do it with a choice
of 11,000 agents—a network of police distributed over
every corner of the land. The Gaelic League, or any
other body in Ireland could not afford the expense
necessary to interview each house-holder. And in
many cases it is very questionable what sort of re-
ception a canvasser would receive in interfering with
the domestic economy of the household. It would
sometimes be a case of bearding the lion in his den—
rather of facing the lioness—although no doubt, in
many cases, a canvasser would be sympathetically re-
ceived. But I fear the idea must be put aside as
being too expensive. You, Sir, in a foot-note, suggest
that the manufacturers might do something more than
they are doing in the matter. Very true. But will
they? The great linen industry is the largest manu-
facturing interest in Ireland. The exports amount to
about 7 millions sterling yearly. Will they trouble can-
vassing each house-holder for orders of which they
already hold a virtual monopoly? Or take the greatest
money-spinners in Ireland—Guinness and Co., Ltd.—
they divided, roundly, a cool million and a quarter ster-
ling as profits last year; will they trouble beating up a
clientéle that they presently hold in leading-strings?
Oh, no. These are large instances. Then there are
many small men, new venturers who have Ilately
““spread experimental sails’’ in small workshops and
mills; they, in many cases (through the apathy of their
countrymen), have enough to do without involving
themselves farther. ~ But there are other substantial
manufacturers, who could have done more in the past
than they have in this matter.

At a meeting held last year in Enniscorthy, it is
stated that ‘‘the bishops have exhorted the clergy to
take a practical interest in the temporal happiness and
welfare of their flocks. This does not, of course, mean
that the pulpit should ever be used for the discussion
of matters purely secular. 'Still where there is a ques-
tion of stopping the spiritual evils of emigration, it is
hard to see how the most spiritual pastor of souls may
not, when speaking against| emigration, incidentally
unfold the theory of supporting Irish industries. In
many instances the Church is the only place the people
can be got together. It should not be con-
sidered inopportune, or inappropriate if the clergy dis-
tribute through the schools, or through the book-
boxes at the church doors, little books bearing on the
question of Irish industries.””  Let that action at
Enniscorthy be made universal over Ireland. Let the
movement embrace the clergy of all denominations. Let
their efforts be persistent, not spasmodic; and there, in
my judgment, you have the remedy for Irish apathy,
if there be a remedy—which at present seems doubtful.

A COUNTRY CHRISTENING.

U E had been down, to tea at the schoolmaster’s,
and the social amenities thereof had been pro-
longed unduly. TFor fully two minutes at the begin-
ning of three successive hours we had protested that we
must be going, indeed we must, and each time had
howed, well-pleased, to a storm of indignant remon-
strance. Now, however, with midnight at hand, with
uneasy thoughts of those who might be waiting up for
us, and with a two-and-a-half-mile walk to face, we
made a fourth protest and bade a reluctant good-bye
to a kindly host and hostess who would have kept us
tiil morning.

““Bright was the night and fair the way,” as we
faced homeward. A harvest moon silvered the upward
road so that it shone whitely. The shadows of hedge
and dyke and tree-trunk bordering the road were as
sharply cut as silhouettes; the little fields stretched out
in dusky shadow or pearl grey light, and over all
reigned a stillness so profound as to seem unearthly to
one used to the murmurous night of towns. There was
just the quiet talk and the sharp footsteps of two men,
the rustle of the trees around a rare haggard, and the
occasional bark, sometimes faint and far-off, sometimes
near, sharp and startling, of dogs that guarded the
geese and kept foxes and tinkers at bay. TFor the rest
there was that wonderful, unspeakable stillness. Under
the white light of the moon the whole countryside was
sunk in a sleep so profound that the night of cities is
a tumult compared with it.

As we neared the Cross of Five Roads we heard in
the distance the rattle of a cart hurriedly driven. Tt
was startling in that ocean of profound peace, and we
waited at the Cross awhile to see who might be ihe
midnight traveller. Presently a cart came swiftly by,
from the driver of which a hasty salutation was flung
at us. He came and went so swiftly that neither of us
recognised him, and his speech did not betray. Tt
was only after we had gone on a hundred yards or more
that my companion gave the exulting cry of the man
who has solved a problem. ‘‘That’s John,”’ said he,
with emphasis. ‘“He’s away off for the nurse and
doctor.”” John was his brother, who had a farm of
land a mile or more beyond that which was home to
me for awhile. He had also a young wife, and was
known to be ' expecting an event—hence my com-

Tha~ was on Thursday midnight; at fom; of th.e clock

I thought it very obliging of him

So far, I had never been to a

my brief holiday made my presence at one certain now~ 
On Friday night his uncle was requisitioned to "stand" 
for him, and I was bidden to the ceremony as a sort 
of supernumerary. 
On Saturday morning the proud 
and flurried father secured audience of the curate, and 
the baptism was fixed for two p.m. on that day at a 
lonely chapel some two or three miles away. 
vVe were 
most earnestly besought to be in good time, for the 
curate had been on the mission in America for many 
years, and was known far and wide as one who preached 
aud practised the strenuous and the punctual life. The 
lii.tter especially-and in Ireland, too! 
In good time the godfather and I set out across the 
fields for the house of the newly born; in good time 
we _reached it, dykes and ditches notwithstanding. But 
in the house there were gathered together many neigh-
bours, and the two graudmoth~rs, and it took some 
time to see and admire the child, to drink its health, 
and the fond mother's health, and more also. 
Thus 
faP. moments sped, though the curate's passion for 
punctuality was spoken of with an awe amounting al-
most to dread. 
Then there was a dispute as to what 
conveyance should take us to the chapel. 
Both father 
aud mother, backed by the godmother, who was aunt 
a., well, urged the charm and the style of the high cart 
with springs. The nurse, however, insisted that the 
common cart, driven slowly, would entail less jolting 
t'J her precious charge, and in the end she had her way. 
It seems to one simple bachelor that the nurse is a very 
high and mighty person on these occasions. Behold 
us, then, mounted thereon' and driven out from the 
yard by the laughing godfather, amid encouraging cries 
from the neighbours, the barking of the two dogs-
who seemed to enter into the festive spirit of the 
occasion-and the loud cackling of a flock of flurried 
geese. 
On one side of the shafts sat the godfather, on 
the other side _myself-with legs dangling and with 
sweet-smelling sops of fresh hay to sit upon. 
I tell 
you " the lady that rides in her carriage might envy " · 
that journey to me. 
On tlie seat, with their backs to 
the horse, the better to shield their treasure from any 
st.ray breath of wind, sat the nurse and the godmother, 
and on the tail sat the father-trying his hardest not 
to look proud and elated. 
The farm, as I have indi-
cated, was up among the hills, but the little chapel was 
higher still. • So our course was upward. A lonely 
road it was and a rough, winding its way over bogs 
and along hill-sides. 
Though the August .day was 
bright with sunshine, the sweet mountain air was as 
nlea:sant about one's face as the touch of soft, cool finger 
tips. Far off in one direction the Galtees could be seen 
like dove-grey t:louds against the blue sky. 
In the 
middle distance a richly wooded valley hid the "fairer 
Blackwater that flows to the sea," and in the fore-
ground were sloping hill-sides, criss-crossed with the 
maze of hedges and ditches so characteristic of an Irish 
countryside. 
That was looking back; forward, we 
faced the purple hill tops, with green fields swarming 
up the slopes in irregular line. 
The grass of mar;'s 
sowing had crept up and up, but the purple heathel' 
still bravely held the crest. 
Along the roadside grew 
foxglove in profusion-the fairy thimble I think they 
call it there-now, alas! with its spikes of bloom fast 
fading. 
But the bell-heather shone redly, and ihe 
delicate seashell pink of the finer heather showed here 
and there, while the yellow gyosadhauns simply flaunted 
themselves. 
It was a scene and a day for light-
heartedness, for .sheer boyish zest and happiness, and 
well we lived up to it. 
No merrier party, I think, 
ever wound its way along that climbing1 road. 
Out of 
mere exuberance the godfather took to whipping up 
the horse t~ntil the car shook again. At first ~he three 
behind were so absorbed in joking conversat10_n as to

His majesty the newcomer was silent, so 
I said nothing-though it occurred to me that a youth

However, 
with the diffidence of the mere bachelor on such occa-
sions I made no protest. At last both nurse and god-

driver and the father cloal<ed his own forgetfulness 
with i~jurious reproaches directed to the s_ame quarter. 
Coufessiug his complete oblivion of the child he was to 
stand for our driver slowed up at once. Thereafter 
until th~ chapel was reached it was_ my duty to 
moderate his ardent use of whip and rems. 
Soon the priest's house came in sight, just bey~nd 
a cr,oss roads garnished by two thatched and white-
washed dispensaries of Beamish's porter. 
A stone's 
t;hrow farther on was the little chapel, a trim, white, 
slat,ecl building with nothing about it to shock one's 
economic sense. 
Inside the vestrv his reverence waited 
for us grimly; we were just ten minutes late. 
The 
reel-shawled woman, who opened the door, lifted her 
eyes and ha,mls silently. 
\Vithout one word she ex-
pressed a wealth of pity; a verse from "The Croppy 
Boy " came into my mind with scraps from the peni-
tential psalms as I looked at her and realised what

Silently we filed in ; 
silently his reverence looked at his watch and then at 
us. 
The godfather broke the silence with an apology

The not quite a day old youngster had Satan

He argued from the Irish at home to the Irish abroad

Reluctantly was he brought 
to believe that the custom of havina their children

Some keep it up; 
many, pe_rhaps most, do n?t. In England, at any rate, 
man:y- Insh people ~all mto the prevailing habit of
